claims: The dataset contains 7199 dialogues with different number of questions each.
To conduct a controlled experiment, this study specifically analyzes the subset of dialogues containing exactly 20 questions.
The LLM used in this research is Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507.
The first approach considers each dialogue separately.
To generate potential questions, the user's dialogue topic is first identified.

To do this, the questions are divided into sections: the first 60% of questions (in this case, 12) will be used to feed the model, while the remaining 40% (8) will be used later as test questions (Fig. 1).
To do this, the previously identified topics and the same 12 questions are fed into the LLM’s promt.
To generate questions, the identified topics, aspects, and the first 60% of questions are passed to the LLM’s prompt.
Finally, at the end, the generated questions are compared with the EQ that weren't passed to the LLM’s prompts.
This is done using a LLM-as-a-judge technique that compares each question to each other.
The comparison uses a scale from 0 to 1, where 0.5 is 'Possibly equivalent (similar intent but different phrasing)'
To simulate this scenario, where we have dialogues with similar topics, 1000 dialogues were considered.
Each of these dialogues also contains exactly 20 questions.
Based on the context of the dialogue, the topic of the dialogue was extracted using LLM.
Next, using DBSCAN (Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise), the topics of the dialogues were grouped into clusters.
First, each cluster is considered separately.
Since the topics of each dialogue have already been defined, the next step is to identify aspects of the topic.
Next, to generate questions, the topics, aspects and PQ of the dialogue under consideration, as well as aspects and PQ of other dialogues in the cluster, are used.
At the end, the same procedure is carried out to compare the generated questions with the EQ for each dialogue separately.
The third approach differs from the previous ones in that it eliminates the step of identifying topic and aspects of the dialogue.
Instead, questions are generated based on the context found in each dialogue in the CoQA dataset.
The dialog context and the PQ are passed to the LLM’s prompt for question generation.
For approaches 1 and 3, 100 identical dialogues with 20 questions were considered.
For the second approach, a total of 85 dialogues were used, grouped into 20 clusters.

To evaluate the effectiveness of each question generation approach, Expected Value (EV) can be calculated: 𝑁 𝐸𝑉 = 1 𝑁 𝑖=1 𝑇𝑖 ∙100% 𝑀 where N is a total number of dialogues, T is a number of successfully predicted test questions in dialogue i, M is a total EQ per dialogue (8 in our case).
